summary:
The Trojan War, traditionally dated to around 1194–1184 BC, is one of the foundational narratives of Greek and Western literary culture, preserved most famously in Homer's Iliad and Odyssey. According to the legend, a coalition of Greek kings led by Agamemnon of Mycenae besieged and ultimately sacked Troy (Ilion) on the northwestern Anatolian coast after a decade of warfare, sparked by the abduction of Helen, wife of Spartan king Menelaus, by the Trojan prince Paris.

The historical basis of the Trojan War remains uncertain. Archaeological excavations at the site of Hisarlik in modern Turkey have revealed a stratified sequence of settlements, and the layer known as Troy VIIa, destroyed by fire around 1180 BC, is the most plausible candidate for the city of Homeric legend. This destruction coincides broadly with the period of widespread upheaval associated with the [[event:1815|Bronze Age Collapse]] and the [[event:1814|Sea Peoples]]. Whether a specific Greek expedition caused this destruction, or whether the epic tradition elaborated a memory of raids and conflicts typical of [[event:57|Mycenaean]] maritime activity, cannot be determined with certainty.

Regardless of its historical basis, the Trojan War tradition had profound cultural consequences. The Iliad and Odyssey, composed in their canonical forms around the eighth century BC, became the central educational texts of the Greek world and anchored a shared mythological universe that united the otherwise fragmented Greek city-states. The war also generated a vast cycle of subsidiary myths — the sacrifice of Iphigenia, the fall of Achilles, the wanderings of Odysseus — that occupied Greek tragedy, visual art, and philosophy for centuries.
